steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.10
Test Case Title: Special characters prefixed in bot commands
Purpose for Agent:
Verify that bot commands are not prefixed with special characters (e.g., "/") and follow Teams bot command guidelines.

Execution Steps for Agent (FOLLOW ORDER)

View Commands Check
Open View Prompts / command list
Check all commands listed
Verify no command starts with special characters (e.g., "/", "!", "#" and any other special character present)

Help Command Check
Send "help"
Review suggested commands/actions
Ensure commands are shown without special character prefixes

Basic Interaction Check
Send commands shown by the bot
Verify commands work without requiring special characters

Prompt/Other Responses Check
Run all available prompts
Check if any command shown includes special character prefixes
Invalid Format Check
Try entering a command with "/" prefix (e.g., "/help")
Verify bot does not enforce or depend on special character usage

Screenshot Capture (evidence):

FIRST, make sure you are inside the bot's 1:1 chat conversation, NOT on the Teams Apps store / app listing / install page. If the current view is the Apps catalog or the app's install/details page, click Open to enter the bot chat before typing anything. The Apps store/listing page must NEVER be used as this test's evidence.
VIEW PROMPTS / command list (observation): Open the bot's View Prompts / command menu, wait for it to fully render and settle, and capture ONE settled screenshot showing the full list of commands so each command's exact text (and any "/", "!", "#", or other special-character prefix) is legible. If the list is taller than the viewport, scroll one section at a time and capture a SEPARATE settled screenshot per position so EVERY command is evidenced.
HELP command (interaction — use the deterministic capture tool): Locate the chat input field and call capture_input_responses with input_field_id = the chat field and inputs = ["help"]. The call types the input, submits it, waits for the bot's RESPONSE to fully render and settle, and captures the settled response. The evidence frame must show the bot's settled suggested commands/actions, NOT the moment of typing. Scroll-per-section with a SEPARATE settled screenshot if the response is taller than the viewport.
BASIC INTERACTION (interaction): For each command shown by the bot, call capture_input_responses with input_field_id = the chat field and inputs = that single command (exactly as the bot displays it, without adding any prefix). Capture each settled response, confirming the command works without requiring a special character.
PROMPT / other responses (interaction): Run each available prompt the same way (one call per prompt) and capture each settled response, checking whether any command shown in those responses includes a special-character prefix.
INVALID FORMAT probe (interaction): Call capture_input_responses with input_field_id = the chat field and inputs = ["/help"], and capture the settled response showing whether the bot enforces or depends on the "/" prefix.
Capture-only guard: Every frame must show the bot's own command list or chat responses. Do NOT substitute the Apps store page, the empty-state landing screen, or unrelated chat content.
These captures must be the LAST actions before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS/FAIL until the View Prompts list, the help response, the executed commands, the prompts, and the "/help" probe have all been captured and settled.
On FAIL, ensure the settled screenshot clearly shows the offending command(s) displayed or required with a special-character prefix (e.g., "/help", "/start") as failure evidence.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame.
Validation Rule:
Bot commands must be simple text commands without any special character prefixes

Expected Behavior:
All commands are displayed and used without special characters
Presence of prefixed commands → FAIL
Test Result:
PASS – No commands use special character prefixes
FAIL – One or more commands include special character prefixes

Failure Details (If Failed):
Example: Commands shown as "/help", "/start"
Example: Bot requires special character prefix to execute commands

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.6
Test Case Name: Error message received for valid commands. Ensure that your bot responses are error-free and provide meaningful guidance to users. Avoid situations where users encounter dead ends due to incorrect or confusing messages.

Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Verify that the bot correctly processes all supported and documented commands without returning system errors, "I don't understand" messages, or technical exceptions. The bot must be reliable for valid inputs.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, the bot must not send an error message—if it responds with a way‑forward guidance message, this is a PASS scenario.
Update: Execute all supported commands via "View Prompts/Suggestions" in the compose tray and via Help response actions (clickable buttons/CTAs/listed commands). Apply the result rules below.
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent (Follow in Order)
1.Click View Prompts and Run All Commands
Open View Prompts / View Suggestions in the compose tray.
type one by one all listed commands under view prompts and inspect all text/cards/error/buttons in the response and capture each response.

Type Help if Help is not listed under "View Prompts" , inspect all text/cards/error/buttons in the response and capture the response. If Help is listed under "View prompts", skip sending Help.
Type all the listed actionables/supported commands(buttons/CTAs/explicit commands), from the help response, if any ,one by one and inspect all text/cards/error/buttons in the response and capture the response.
3.) Type Generic Commands
Send hi and hello; inspect all text/cards/error/buttons in the response and capture the response.

Result Evaluation Logic
PASS
You receive valid bot responses (meaningful result or way‑forward guidance; no system/fallback errors) for all items executed across Steps 1–3.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, way‑forward guidance (e.g., "Please connect your account to use this command") is acceptable and counts as valid.
FAIL
A valid bot response is not received (silence/no reply) for any executed item, or
A system/technical error appears (e.g., "Internal Server Error", "Bot encountered an error", "Operation failed", stack traces), or
The bot replies with unrecognized/fallback for a command surfaced in View Prompts or Help-listed actionables (e.g., "I don't recognize that", "I don't understand").
WARNING
All responses are valid (no errors/silence), but multiple different executed items (any from Steps 1–3) receive the same bot response (materially identical text/card/intent/CTA).
Use this standard warning text:
"Same response received for multiple supported commands/actions; kindly review the responses."
Notes for Consistency
Treat Help text itself as informational; only run the actionables/supported commands exposed in Help.
Consider messages the same when the content/intent/CTAs match (ignore timestamps/IDs).

Screenshot Capture (evidence):

FIRST, make sure you are inside the bot's 1:1 chat conversation, NOT on the Teams Apps store / app listing / install page. If the current view is the Apps catalog or the app's install/details page, click Open to enter the bot chat before capturing. The Apps store/listing page must NEVER be used as this test's evidence.
View Prompts list: Open View Prompts / View Suggestions in the compose tray, wait for the list to settle, and capture ONE screenshot of the full list of suggested commands before running them.
This is an interaction capture (use the deterministic capture tool): For EACH command — every View-Prompts item, then Help (only if not listed under View Prompts), then each Help-listed actionable, then "hi" and "hello" — locate the chat input field and call capture_input_responses with input_field_id = the chat field and inputs = that single command. Each call types the input, submits it, waits for the bot's RESPONSE to fully render and settle, and captures the settled response — so each evidence frame shows the reply, not the moment of typing. Capture EACH command's response as its own distinct, settled screenshot.
If a response is taller than the viewport, scroll one section at a time and capture a SEPARATE settled screenshot per position so all text/cards/error states/buttons are evidenced.
Capture-only guard: Every frame must show the bot's own response to the specific command in the chat. Do NOT substitute the Apps store page, the empty-state landing screen, or unrelated content; keep each command paired with its own response frame so distinct vs identical responses can be compared.
These captures must be the LAST actions before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS / FAIL / WARNING until every executed item (View Prompts + Help actionables + generic) has its own settled response screenshot.
On FAIL, ensure the settled screenshot clearly shows the system/technical error, the unrecognized/fallback reply, or the absence of any reply (silence) for the specific command as failure evidence.
On WARNING, ensure the screenshots place the materially identical responses side by side (same text/card/intent/CTA across different commands) as warning evidence.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame.
Expected Result
The bot must correctly process all valid commands (from " View Prompts" and Help-listed actions).
No system errors or unrecognized command responses should appear.
PASS if every executed item responds validly (including way‑forward guidance for Non‑Integrated).
FAIL if any executed item yields error/silence/unrecognized.
WARNING if responses are valid but identical across multiple different executed items.

Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: PASS / FAIL / WARNING
Result Conditions Recap:
✅ PASS
Valid responses for all commands/actions executed ( view prompts + Help actions + generic), no errors.
❌ FAIL
Any command/action (from prompts or Help) returns error or unrecognized, or no response.
⚠️ WARNING
Valid responses are received, but the same response is observed across multiple different executed items.
Use: "Same response received for multiple supported commands/actions; kindly review the responses."
Failure/Warning Reasons (If applicable)
FAIL Example(s):
"The command 'My Profile' returned a technical error."
"Valid command from prompts returned 'Command not found'."
"No response for 'Create Ticket' from View Prompts."
WARNING Example:
"Same response received for multiple supported commands/actions; kindly review the responses."
Evidence (attach)
View Prompts list
Each command execution from prompts and its response
Help response (if executed) showing actionables
Each Help-listed actionable execution and its response
Generic (hi/hello) responses
A comparison highlighting identical vs distinct responses

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.1.2
Test Case Name: Multi turn bot response for repetitive tasks. Implement task module to avoid multi turn bot response when the workflow involves the user completing repetitive tasks (eg: use a task module to repetitively capture name, dob, place, designation instead of using multi turn conversations).

Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Verify that the bot utilizes a Task Module (pop-up modal) to collect repetitive or complex data (Name, DOB, Place, Designation) instead of long multi-turn chat.
For Non-Integrated accounts, check whether the bot either:
Sends multiple chat questions OR Opens a Task Module
If neither happens and the bot gives only one clear guidance message, the scenario is still PASS.
Updated Logic:
Before all steps, the agent must first check supported bot commands under "View prompts" from the compose tray and run all available commands to review app behavior.
If no commands are found, proceed with the original steps.
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:
Check Supported Commands First:
Open "View prompts" from the compose tray.
Run all suggested commands that relate to workflows requiring multiple data inputs.
Observe the bot's behavior (Task Module vs. multi-turn chat).
If No Commands Are Available:
Proceed with the below logic:
Trigger Workflow:
Initiate a feature that requires multiple data points (e.g., "Create Profile," "Register User").
Monitor Interaction Method:
Check if the bot opens a Task Module or sends a series of individual chat questions.
Evaluate Efficiency:
Confirm the Task Module allows entering all fields (Name, DOB, Place, Designation) in one modal interface.
Check Redundancy:
Ensure the bot does not revert to unnecessary multi-turn chat when data can be collected in UI.
Validate Non-Integrated Behavior:
For Non-Integrated accounts, multiple chat questions or Task Module are both acceptable.
If neither happens and the bot sends only one guidance message, count the result as PASS.

Screenshot Capture (evidence):

FIRST, make sure you are inside the bot's 1:1 chat conversation, NOT on the Teams Apps store / app listing / install page. If the current view is the Apps catalog or the app's install/details page, click Open to enter the bot chat before capturing. The Apps store/listing page must NEVER be used as this test's evidence.
View Prompts list: Open View Prompts / View Suggestions in the compose tray, wait for the list to settle, and capture ONE screenshot of the listed commands before running them.
This is an interaction capture (use the deterministic capture tool): For each workflow command (a relevant View-Prompts item, or a trigger like "Create Profile" / "Register User" if no commands are listed), locate the chat input field and call capture_input_responses with input_field_id = the chat field and inputs = that single command. Each call types the input, submits it, waits for the bot's RESPONSE to fully render and settle, and captures the settled result.
TASK MODULE evidence (PASS path): If the bot opens a Task Module (pop-up modal), wait for the modal to fully render and settle, then capture ONE screenshot of the modal showing all input fields (e.g., Name, DOB, Place, Designation) collected in a single interface. If the modal is taller than the viewport, scroll one section at a time and capture a SEPARATE settled screenshot per position so all fields are evidenced.
MULTI-TURN evidence (FAIL path in integrated scenarios): If the bot instead asks a series of individual chat questions, capture the settled chat thread showing the separate consecutive questions as evidence of multi-turn collection. If the questions do not fit one viewport, scroll and capture a SEPARATE settled screenshot per segment so the full sequence is evidenced.
SINGLE-GUIDANCE evidence (Non-Integrated PASS): If the bot sends only one consolidated guidance message and neither a Task Module nor multi-turn chat occurs, capture ONE settled screenshot of that single guidance response.
Capture-only guard: Every frame must show the bot's own chat response, the genuine Task Module modal, or the View Prompts list. Do NOT substitute the Apps store page, the empty-state landing screen, or unrelated chat content from other test steps.
These captures must be the LAST actions before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS/FAIL until the interaction method (Task Module, multi-turn chat, or single guidance message) has been fully captured and settled.
On FAIL, ensure the settled screenshots clearly show the multiple separate chat questions (integrated scenario) where a Task Module should have been used, as failure evidence.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame.
Expected Result:
The bot must open a Task Module for repetitive data fields in integrated scenarios.
For Non-Integrated accounts, multiple chat questions or a Task Module are acceptable.
A single clear guidance message is also valid.

Final Execution Report
Test Case Result:
[PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: "The bot asked 4 separate questions in the chat thread in an integrated scenario. Suggested correction: Use a Task Module to collect Name, DOB, Place, and Designation in one form." "Suggested command from View Prompts triggered multi-turn chat instead of Task Module."
Evidence:
[Insert Screenshot of chat vs. Task Module]

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.12
Test Case Name: Providing same response to both valid and invalid command

Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure the bot distinguishes between valid and invalid commands with specific, context‑aware responses. The bot should not trigger its default "Help" or "Fallback" response for recognized commands.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, if the bot gives the same response for both valid and invalid commands but includes a clear way‑forward guidance message, treat as WARNING (not Pass/Fail).

Detailed Execution Steps for Agent (ENFORCED ORDER)

View Prompts — Discover & Run All Supported Commands
Click View prompts / View suggestions in the compose tray.
Run every listed command and record each response (text/cards/CTAs/intent).
Save the command → response pairs for comparison.
Help Command (Conditional)
If "Help" is listed in View Prompts → skip this step.
Otherwise, send help and record the response.
If the Help response mentions supported actions/commands (buttons/CTAs/listed commands), capture those items (names/labels/IDs) for Step 3.

Run Supported Actions Listed in Help (If Present)
Execute all supported actions/commands surfaced in the Help response (buttons/CTAs/listed commands).
Record each response.
Save the action → response pairs for comparison.

Generic Command
Send one generic input (e.g., hi or hello).
Record the response.

Invalid Command
Send one invalid input (e.g., xyz123).
Record the response.
Comparison & Decision Logic (APPLY EXACTLY AS BELOW)

Similarity Rule: "Same response" means materially identical text/cards/intent/CTAs (minor timestamp or message ID differences do not matter).

A) WARNING Condition

If any two responses among Steps 1–4 (Prompts, Help* if executed, Help‑listed actions, Generic) are the same,
→ WARNING with this message:
"Same configuration/setup message received for the commands. Kindly review the message."
(Examples: generic = same as prompts; prompts = same as help‑listed action; etc.)

B) FAIL Condition

If the Invalid command (Step 5) response matches any response from Steps 1–4,
→ FAIL and list explicitly which commands matched (e.g., "Invalid 'xyz123' matched response of 'Check Status' and 'Create Task'").
(This indicates valid and invalid are treated identically.)

C) PASS Condition

If no WARNING condition is triggered and no FAIL condition is triggered,
→ PASS (valid commands are distinct and invalid gets a fallback/guidance that does not duplicate valid/generic/help responses).

Note (Non‑Integrated accounts):
If the bot returns the same way‑forward guidance for both valid and invalid inputs, classify per above logic:

If Step 5 (invalid) matches any of Steps 1–4 → FAIL
Else if any matches within Steps 1–4 → WARNING
Else PASS

Screenshot Capture (evidence):

FIRST, make sure you are inside the bot's 1:1 chat conversation, NOT on the Teams Apps store / app listing / install page. If the current view is the Apps catalog or the app's install/details page, click Open to enter the bot chat before typing anything. The Apps store/listing page must NEVER be used as this test's evidence.
VIEW PROMPTS list (Step 1 — observation): Open View Prompts / View Suggestions in the compose tray, wait for it to fully render and settle, and capture ONE settled screenshot of the full command list. Scroll-per-section with a SEPARATE settled screenshot if it is taller than the viewport.
EACH COMMAND / HELP / HELP-LISTED ACTION / GENERIC / INVALID (Steps 1–5 — interaction, use the deterministic capture tool): For every input under test — each View Prompts command, then ["help"] (only if Help is not already listed in View Prompts), then each Help‑listed action one by one, then one generic input (["hi"] or ["hello"]), then one invalid input (["xyz123"]) — locate the chat input field and call capture_input_responses with input_field_id = the chat field and inputs = that single command. Each call types the input, submits it, waits for the bot's RESPONSE to fully render and settle, and captures the settled response. The evidence frame must show the settled response (text/card/CTA), NOT the moment of typing.
Capture each command → response as its OWN settled screenshot, with the input and its reply both visible, so the command↔response pairing is unambiguous for the comparison logic. If any response is taller than the viewport, scroll one section at a time and capture a SEPARATE settled screenshot per position.
COMPARISON evidence: After all responses are captured, capture/assemble the frames needed to show which responses are materially identical vs distinct (especially: the invalid "xyz123" response side‑by‑side with any Step 1–4 response it matches, and any two Step 1–4 responses that match each other).
Capture-only guard: Every frame must show the bot's own command list or chat responses. Do NOT substitute the Apps store page, the empty-state landing screen, or unrelated chat content.
These captures must be the LAST actions before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS/FAIL/WARNING until the View Prompts list and every Step 1–5 response have been captured and settled.
On FAIL, ensure the settled screenshots clearly show the invalid‑command response matching a specific valid/generic/help response (name the matched commands). On WARNING, ensure the screenshots show the two or more Step 1–4 responses that are identical.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame.
Expected Result:
Valid command(s) → trigger correct workflows/actions.
Invalid command → receives fallback/guidance that is not identical to any valid/generic/help/help‑listed action response.
Warnings flag sameness among valid/generic/help/help‑listed action buckets (Steps 1–4).
Fail flags sameness between invalid (Step 5) and any of Steps 1–4.

Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: PASS / FAIL / WARNING
Result Guidelines:

PASS

Distinct responses across: prompts commands, help/help‑listed actions (if run), generic;
Invalid command response differs from all others.

FAIL

Invalid (Step 5) response matches any response from Steps 1–4 (name all commands/actions that matched), or a valid command incorrectly triggers fallback/help.

WARNING
Any two or more among Steps 1–4 produce the same response.
Use: "Same configuration/setup message received for the commands. Kindly review the message."

Failure/Warning Reasons (If applicable):

FAIL Example: "Invalid 'xyz123' produced the same response as 'Check Status' and 'Create Task'."
WARNING Example: "Responses for 'hi' and 'Check Status' are identical. Same configuration/setup message received for the commands. Kindly review the message."

Evidence:
Attach screenshots of:

View prompts list
Each prompted command execution & response
Help response (if executed) and help‑listed actions (if any) & their responses
Generic command response
Invalid command response
A comparison highlighting identical vs distinct responses

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.2.4
Test Case Title: App name mismatch between manifest app name and bot welcome message/bot responses

Purpose for Agent:
Verify that the app name shown in the bot welcome message and all responses matches exactly with the app name defined in the manifest. If the app name in the welcome message or any bot response does not match the manifest app name, the test fails.

Execution Steps for Agent (FOLLOW ORDER)

App Name Verification
Open app details
Note the app name from the manifest

Welcome Message Check
Trigger/reset welcome message
Verify the displayed app/bot name matches manifest name

Basic Interaction Check
Send "hi", "hello"
Check if bot responses use the correct app name
Ensure no different name appears

Help Command Check
Send "help"
Verify app name used in response matches manifest

Prompt/Other Responses Check
Run View Prompts (if available)
Check all responses for consistent app name usage

Screenshot Capture (evidence — one screenshot per step below):
Step 1 (Manifest) — observation: WAIT until the app details are fully settled/loaded, then capture ONE settled screenshot showing the app name in the manifest/app details.
Step 2 (Welcome) — observation: WAIT until the page is settled and the Welcome message is fully rendered, then capture ONE settled screenshot showing the displayed app/bot name.
Step 3 (hi/hello) — interaction: Locate the chat input field, then call capture_input_responses with input_field_id = the chat field and inputs: ["hi", "hello"]. This submits each greeting one at a time and captures a SEPARATE settled screenshot of each bot reply (showing the app name) — so the evidence shows the responses, not the moment the messages were typed.
Step 4 (help) — interaction: Call capture_input_responses with input_field_id = the chat field and inputs: ["help"] to submit "help" and capture the settled Help response showing the app name used.
Step 5 (View Prompts / other) — observation: Run View Prompts (if available), WAIT until each response renders and settles, then capture ONE settled screenshot per response showing the app name.
The final response screenshot must be the LAST action before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS/FAIL until every step above has its settled screenshot captured.
On FAIL, ensure the screenshot clearly shows the mismatched/incorrect app name as failure evidence.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame.

Validation Rule:
App name must be identical in manifest, welcome message, and all bot responses

Expected Behavior:
Bot always uses the same app name as defined in manifest
Any mismatch in naming → FAIL

Test Result:
PASS – App name is consistent everywhere
FAIL – App name mismatch found

Failure Details (If Failed):
Example: Welcome message shows different app name
Example: Bot responses use old or incorrect app name

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.8
Test Case Name: Help command redirecting outside Teams. Bot help command response must not redirect user outside of Teams. Bot help command response can redirect user to a canvas within the Teams app or provide a way forward response in an adaptive card.

Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure that the response to the "help" command keeps the user within the Microsoft Teams environment. If the help command response redirects the user outside Teams as its primary action, the test fails. Documentation and instructions must be delivered in‑app rather than forcing an external redirect. Bot responses may include hyperlinks for way‑forward guidance or contact; if hovering over such links shows a hand cursor, this is acceptable and is a PASS scenario for Non‑Integrated accounts.

Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:
Trigger Help:
Send help (or select Help from the command menu).
Analyze Response Format:
Confirm the bot replies inside Teams with a message/card.
Inspect Links (Updated):
Read the help response and classify it strictly into one of the below:
Way‑forward guidance / setup message (e.g., steps to connect account, configuration notes, how to proceed), or
In‑app workflow / list of actionable items / supported commands (buttons, CTAs, enumerated commands)
Then:
Detect if any hyperlink text exists and whether labels include external indicators (e.g., Open in Browser, View in Web Portal, ↗️).
Hover over the hyperlink(s) to confirm hand cursor behavior.
Verify Redirection:
Help must not rely solely on opening an external website as the primary action.
Check Canvas Usage:
If a canvas is opened, ensure it loads in Teams (Task Module or Tab), not a browser.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, sharing an external link as way‑forward guidance is valid.

Screenshot Capture (evidence):
Help response (interaction — use the deterministic capture tool): Locate the chat input field, then call capture_input_responses with input_field_id = the chat field and inputs: ["help"]. This submits "help", waits for the help RESPONSE message/card to fully render and settle, and captures it automatically — so the evidence frame shows the help reply, NOT the moment "help" was typed.
If the response is taller than the viewport, scroll through it one section at a time and, after each scroll, wait for the view to settle and capture an ADDITIONAL screenshot so the full content — guidance/setup text or the in‑app workflow/list of actionable items/supported commands — is captured.
Capture a settled screenshot showing any hyperlink(s) in the response, and where observable, hover over a hyperlink, wait for the view to settle, then capture a screenshot of the hand‑cursor hover state.
If a canvas/Task Module/Tab opens, wait for it to settle, then capture a screenshot showing where it loads (inside Teams vs. a browser). If an external redirect occurs, capture a settled screenshot of that redirection behavior.
The help-response screenshot must be the LAST action before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS/FAIL/WARNING until the rendered help response (and any link/hover/redirect evidence) has been captured.
On FAIL, ensure the screenshot shows the external-redirect-as-primary-action behavior. On WARNING, ensure the screenshot clearly shows the hyperlink text that lacks an external indicator.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame.
Expected Result:
Help response must be delivered in‑app.
Either way‑forward guidance/setup is provided, or an in‑app workflow/list of actionables is present.
External links can appear as supplementary guidance.
Hyperlinks showing a hand cursor on hover are compliant.

Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL / WARNING]
Result Conditions:

✅ PASS
Step 1 or 2 is met:
The help response is way‑forward guidance/setup, or
The help response lists in‑app workflows/actionable items/supported commands,
and does not force the user outside Teams as the primary action.

❌ FAIL
The help response concludes by asking the user to do something outside Teams as the primary (or only) action (e.g., immediately opens a browser or only provides an external link without in‑app summary/workflow).

⚠️ WARNING
Step 1 or 2 qualifies for PASS, but the same help response contains a hyperlink text without an external indicator in its label.
Warning message:
"List of actions/supported commands/way‑forward guidance/configuration message contains a hyperlink without an external indicator. Kindly review the bot response."

Failure/Warning Reasons (If applicable):
FAIL Example:
"Sending 'help' opened a browser immediately. Provide a help summary inside Teams."
WARNING Example:
"List of actions/supported commands/way‑forward guidance/configuration message contains a hyperlink without an external indicator. Kindly review the bot response."

Evidence:
[Insert screenshots showing the help response, the presence of actionables or guidance, any links, hover state (hand cursor), and any redirection behavior]

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.2
Test Case Name: Bot does not respond to any of the user commands

Objective:
To verify that the bot remains active and responsive across all supported scopes (Personal, Channel, Group Chat). If the bot does not respond to one or more user commands (silence/no reply to any input in Steps 2–6), the test fails.
The bot must handle:
Generic Commands (e.g., "Hi", "Hello")
Standard Commands (e.g., "Help")
Invalid Commands (e.g., "xyz123") with a way‑forward message

UPDATE:
❗ WARNING logic removed — only PASS or FAIL results.
❗ Non‑Integrated Exception: If the bot consistently returns the same setup/way‑forward guidance message for all inputs (including invalid), this should be treated as PASS for Non‑Integrated accounts (as long as a clear way‑forward is present and the bot is responsive).

Detailed Execution Steps:

Installation:
Ensure the bot is installed in the selected scope.

View Prompts (Run All):
Click View Prompts / View Suggestions in the compose tray.
Run all commands listed and observe/capture each response.

Screenshot Capture (evidence — one screenshot per executed command/response):

Step 2 (View Prompts) — dynamic, settle-anchored: Capture ONE settled screenshot of the View Prompts / View Suggestions list. Then run each listed command (these are discovered at runtime, so run them individually); after running EACH command, WAIT until that command's RESPONSE is fully rendered and settled, then capture a screenshot of each response.
Step 3 (Help, if executed) — interaction: Only if Help is NOT listed in View Prompts, locate the chat input field and call capture_input_responses with input_field_id = the chat field and inputs: ["Help"] to submit "Help" and capture the settled help response.
Step 4 (Help actionables, if present) — dynamic, settle-anchored: If the Help response contains actionable items/supported commands, run each one (clicked/triggered at runtime); after EACH, WAIT for its response to render and settle, then capture a screenshot of each.
Step 5 (Hi / Hello) — interaction: Locate the chat input field and call capture_input_responses with input_field_id = the chat field and inputs: ["Hi", "Hello"] to submit each greeting and capture a SEPARATE settled response screenshot of each.
Step 6 (xyz123) — interaction: Call capture_input_responses with input_field_id = the chat field and inputs: ["xyz123"] to submit the invalid command and capture the settled fallback/guidance response.
If any response is taller than the viewport, scroll through it one section at a time and, after each scroll, wait for the view to settle and capture an ADDITIONAL screenshot so the full content is captured.
The Step 6 (invalid command) response screenshot must be the LAST action before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS/FAIL until every executed item in Steps 2–6 has its settled response screenshot captured.
On FAIL, ensure the screenshot clearly shows the failing item — silence/no reply, the identical generic message (Integrated), a Help fallback/"unrecognized", or an incorrect invalid-command response — as failure evidence.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame.
Help Command (Conditional):
If Help is not listed in View Prompts, send "Help" and capture the response.
If Help is listed in prompts, skip sending Help here.

Help Actionables (if present):
If the Help response contains actionable items/supported commands (buttons/CTAs/listed commands), run each of them and capture responses.

Generic Commands:
Send "Hi" and "Hello"; capture responses.

Invalid Command:

Pass/Fail trigger for Step 6:

Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.17
Test Case Name: Bot does not respond to help command typed in lowercase and/or uppercase. Bots must provide a valid response to "help" command typed in lowercase or uppercase.
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Verify that the bot correctly recognizes and responds to the "help" command regardless of casing (lowercase, uppercase, or mixed). If the bot does not respond (or returns an error) to the "help" command typed in lowercase, uppercase, or mixed case, the test fails. The bot must not fail or show an error because of capitalization. Hyperlinks inside help responses are allowed, and if the cursor changes to a hand icon when hovered over them, it indicates valid external redirection.

Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:
Test Lowercase: Type help and observe the response.
Test Uppercase: Type HELP and observe the response.
Test Mixed Case: Type Help or hElP and observe the response.
Compare Consistency: Ensure all variations return the same help resource (help card, feature list, or support link).
Check for Dead Ends: Confirm no variation triggers an invalid‑command error. For Non‑Integrated accounts, if all variations return the same way‑forward guidance message, this is a PASS scenario.

Screenshot Capture (evidence — one screenshot per casing variation):

Casing variations (interaction — use the deterministic capture tool): Locate the chat input field, then call capture_input_responses with input_field_id = the chat field and inputs: ["help", "HELP", "hElP"]. This submits each casing variation one at a time, waits for each response to fully render and settle, and captures a SEPARATE settled screenshot of each response — so every casing has its own evidence frame showing the actual reply (not the moment the command was typed). The inputs must be submitted in this exact order so the lowercase, uppercase, and mixed-case responses are captured individually.
If any response is taller than the viewport, scroll through it one section at a time and, after each scroll, wait for the view to settle and capture an ADDITIONAL screenshot so the full help resource is captured.
Where a hyperlink is present, hover over it, wait for the view to settle, then capture a screenshot of the hand‑cursor hover state where observable.
The final variation's response screenshot must be the LAST action before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS/FAIL until all three casing variations have their settled response screenshots captured.
On FAIL, ensure the screenshot clearly shows the variation that returned an invalid‑command error or a different response as failure evidence.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame.
Expected Result:
The bot must treat all casing variations of "help" the same and return a proper help response. Hyperlinks with hand‑cursor hover are compliant.

Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: "The bot responded to 'help' correctly but returned an 'unrecognized command' for 'HELP'. Suggested correction: make command matching case‑insensitive."
Evidence: [Insert Screenshot of bot failing to recognize a casing variation]

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.4
Test Case Name: Bot response does not provide way-forward guidance. Ensure your bot responses guide users effectively. Avoid dead ends; always provide a way forward.

Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure that every interaction with the bot provides the user with a clear next step or "way forward." The bot must never leave the user in a dead-end state after completing a task, receiving empty search results, or hitting a logical stop. Bot responses may contain hyperlinks (e.g., contact links, help docs, or way‑forward links). If hovering over a hyperlink changes the cursor to a hand icon, indicating external navigation, this is a PASS scenario.

Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:
Trigger Completion States: Complete a workflow (e.g., "Submit Expense Report," "Delete Task").
Trigger Empty States: Search for a term with no results.
Inspect Response UI: Observe the final bot message in these conditions.
Identify Guidance: Look for:
Suggested Actions (e.g., "Back to Menu," "Try Again," "Create New").
Text Hints (e.g., "Type 'Help' to see more options").
Hyperlinks that show a hand cursor on hover, confirming navigation and serving as way‑forward guidance.
Check for Dead Ends: Ensure the bot does not stop at "Deleted." or "No results." without any follow-up options.
Screenshot Capture (evidence):

FIRST, make sure you are inside the bot's 1:1 chat conversation, NOT on the Teams Apps store / app listing / install page. If the current view is the Apps catalog or the app's install/details page, click Open to enter the bot chat before capturing. The Apps store/listing page must NEVER be used as this test's evidence.
This is an interaction capture (use the deterministic capture tool): For each state under test, locate the chat input field and call capture_input_responses with input_field_id = the chat field and inputs = the command that triggers it (e.g., a completion workflow command, then a no-results search term). Each call types the input, submits it, waits for the bot's RESPONSE to fully render and settle, and captures the settled response — so the evidence frame shows the final bot message, not the moment of typing.
The KEY evidence frame is the FINAL bot message in each state: after the response has fully rendered and settled, capture ONE screenshot showing the complete message including any suggested-action buttons, text hints, or way‑forward links (or their absence). If the message is taller than the viewport, scroll one section at a time and capture a SEPARATE settled screenshot per position so all buttons/links/hints are evidenced.
Capture BOTH states: (a) the completion/task-done response, and (b) the empty/no-results response — each as its own settled screenshot, because dead-ends can occur in either.
HOVER state (where applicable): If a way‑forward hyperlink is present, hover over it, wait for the view to settle, then capture a separate screenshot showing the cursor as a hand icon over the link (external-navigation evidence).
Capture-only guard: Every frame must show the bot's own response in the chat. Do NOT substitute the Apps store page, the empty-state landing screen, or unrelated chat content from other test steps.
These captures must be the LAST actions before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS/FAIL until the final bot message in BOTH the completion and empty states (and the hover state, where applicable) has been captured and settled.
On FAIL, ensure the settled screenshot clearly shows the dead-end response (e.g., "Deleted." or "No results.") with NO follow-up button, hint, or link as failure evidence.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame.
Expected Result:
The bot must always provide a way forward. Every message must invite further interaction or offer buttons/links for next steps. Hyperlinks used for guidance are compliant if the cursor displays a hand icon on hover.
Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: "After updating the profile, the bot replied 'Profile Updated' with no next action. Suggested correction: Add 'View Profile' or 'Back to Menu' guidance."
Example: "'No results found' did not include retry options. Suggested correction: Add 'Search Again' or help suggestions."
Evidence: [Insert screenshot of response lacking next‑step guidance]

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.9
Test Case Name: Irrelevant, improper, unresponsive response to bot command. Ensure your bot acknowledges all user inputs, even if they are irrelevant or improper.

Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Verify that the bot remains responsive and professional when faced with irrelevant, improper, or nonsensical user inputs. The bot must never remain silent and must avoid technical errors, raw code, or unhandled exception messages. For Non‑Integrated accounts, if the bot provides the same response for all commands but includes a way‑forward guidance message, this is a PASS scenario.

Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:
Test Nonsensical Input: Type random characters (e.g., "asdfghjkl") and confirm the bot acknowledges it.
Test Out‑of‑Scope Requests: Ask something the bot is not designed to handle (e.g., weather queries).
Test Improper Language: Send slang or unclear phrasing (within professional limits) and verify the bot maintains a polite fallback tone.
Check for Technical Leakage: Ensure no raw JSON, undefined variables, or empty bubbles appear.
Validate Acknowledgment: Confirm the bot uses a graceful fallback that guides the user to valid commands. For Non‑Integrated accounts, a repeated guidance response is acceptable.

Screenshot Capture (evidence):
FIRST, make sure you are inside the bot's 1:1 chat conversation, NOT on the Teams Apps store / app listing / install page. If the current view is the Apps catalog or the app's install/details page, click Open to enter the bot chat before capturing. The Apps store/listing page must NEVER be used as this test's evidence.
This is an interaction capture (use the deterministic capture tool): For EACH input under test — nonsensical (e.g., ["asdfghjkl"]), out-of-scope (e.g., ["What's the weather today?"]), and improper/unclear phrasing — locate the chat input field and call capture_input_responses with input_field_id = the chat field and inputs = that single input. Each call types the input, submits it, waits for the bot's RESPONSE to fully render and settle, and captures the settled reply — so each evidence frame shows the bot's fallback, not the moment of typing.
The KEY evidence frame for each input is the bot's settled RESPONSE: capture ONE screenshot per input showing the complete acknowledgment/fallback message including any way‑forward guidance (or its absence). If a response is taller than the viewport, scroll one section at a time and capture a SEPARATE settled screenshot per position.
Silence check (for FAIL): If the bot does NOT reply to an input, wait for the view to settle and capture ONE screenshot showing the user's input with no bot response following it (and no empty/blank bubble resolving) as evidence of silence.
Technical-leakage check: Where present, ensure the settled screenshot clearly captures any raw JSON, undefined variables, stack traces, error strings, or empty bubbles as failure evidence.
Capture-only guard: Every frame must show the bot's own response (or its absence) to the specific input in the chat. Do NOT substitute the Apps store page, the empty-state landing screen, or unrelated chat content from other test steps; keep each input paired with its own response frame.
These captures must be the LAST actions before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS/FAIL until each tested input (nonsensical, out-of-scope, improper) has its own settled response screenshot — or, on failure, a settled silence/technical-leakage screenshot.
On FAIL, ensure the settled screenshot clearly shows either the bot's silence or the technical/error output (e.g., "Error: Intent not found", raw JSON) as failure evidence.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame.
Expected Result:
The bot must always acknowledge user input. A compliant response politely states that the input isn't recognized and provides a way‑forward option. Silence or incomplete technical output is non‑compliant. For Non‑Integrated accounts, identical guidance for all commands qualifies as PASS.
Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: "The bot remained silent when the user typed 'Hello bot'. Suggested correction: add a default fallback response."
Example: "The bot returned 'Error: Intent not found' to an irrelevant query. Suggested correction: replace with a user‑friendly fallback."
Evidence: [Insert screenshot of the bot failing to respond or displaying technical output]

Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.11
Test Case Name: Error message for invalid commands. Bots must provide a valid response to invalid user commands. Bots must not dead end the user or display an error if a user sends an invalid bot command.
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure the bot handles unrecognized or invalid inputs gracefully by providing a helpful response (such as a help menu or a list of valid commands) rather than remaining silent ("dead‑ending") or displaying a technical error message. For Non‑Integrated accounts, verify that the bot does not return an error and instead provides a way‑forward guidance message; this should be treated as a PASS scenario.
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:
Initiate Interaction: Open the chat interface with the bot.
Send Invalid Command: Type a random or unsupported string (e.g., "xyz123" or "invalid_command") and send it.
Observe Response: Monitor the bot's reaction to the unrecognized input.
Verify Content: Check if the response includes guidance such as a help card, suggested commands, or a polite clarification message.
Check for Dead Ends: Ensure the bot does not fail to reply or show technical errors. For Non‑Integrated accounts, confirm that a way‑forward guidance message is shown instead of an error—this is PASS.
Screenshot Capture (evidence):

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.6.2
Test Case Name: Bot sends multiple messages in quick succession.

Objective:
To verify that the bot consolidates its responses into a single, cohesive message rather than sending multiple messages in quick succession. This ensures users are not overwhelmed with multiple notifications and keeps the chat history organized. For Non‑Integrated accounts, if the bot responds with a single guidance message as its default behavior, this is considered a PASS scenario.

Detailed Execution Steps:
Trigger a Complex Response: Send a command that typically returns multi‑part information (e.g., "Get Full Status" or "Help").
Monitor Notification Count: Observe the Teams activity feed and the chat window.
Count Message Bubbles: Count how many distinct messages the bot sends for that one query.
Evaluate Structure:
Fail: Bot sends multiple bubbles such as: "Hello" → "Here is your data" → a Table.
Pass: Bot sends ONE Adaptive Card containing all sections (greeting, data, table).
Assess Logic: Ensure the bot uses logical visual structure (containers, sections, dividers) within the single message. For Non‑Integrated accounts, if one consolidated way‑forward response is provided, it is valid.

Screenshot Capture (evidence):

FIRST, make sure you are inside the bot's 1:1 chat conversation, NOT on the Teams Apps store / app listing / install page. If the current view is the Apps catalog or the app's install/details page, click Open to enter the bot chat before capturing. The Apps store/listing page must NEVER be used as this test's evidence, and its store tiles/cards must NEVER be counted as bot messages.
This is an interaction capture (use the deterministic capture tool): Locate the chat input field, then call capture_input_responses with input_field_id = the chat field and inputs = a multi‑part command (e.g., ["Get Full Status"] or ["Help"]). This types the command, submits it, waits for the bot's RESPONSE to fully render and settle, and captures the settled result.
After the response, give the conversation a few extra seconds so that ALL bubbles for that single query (including any delayed second/third message) have arrived and rendered before finalizing the count. Do not finalize while messages are still streaming in — a premature frame can under-count the bubbles.
Count-only guard: Count and capture ONLY the bot bubbles produced in reply to THIS single command. Capture ONE settled screenshot that makes the TOTAL bubble count for the one query clear (the user's command followed by all bot bubbles it triggered). Do NOT count the empty-state landing screen, store tiles, or responses to other commands.
If the bubbles do not all fit in one viewport, scroll through the conversation one segment at a time. After EACH scroll, wait for the view to settle, then capture a SEPARATE screenshot so every distinct bot bubble for that query is captured. Every scroll position must be its own distinct, settled screenshot.
These captures must be the LAST actions before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS/FAIL until the full bubble count for the single query is evidenced.
On FAIL (two or more bubbles for one query), ensure the settled screenshots clearly show all the separate consecutive bot messages produced by that single command as failure evidence.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame.
Expected Result:
The bot must return the entire response in one single message.
The content should use Markdown or Adaptive Cards for readability.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, a single guidance message is acceptable and should PASS.
Final Execution Report:

Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.6.2
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons:
Example: "Bot sent four separate messages in 2 seconds for a single Status command. All content must be consolidated."
User Impact: Multiple notifications cause "Notification Fatigue," leading users to mute or uninstall the bot.
Evidence: (Screenshot showing multiple consecutive bot messages for one request.)

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.15
Test Case Name: Shows typing indicator after responding to user. Bots must not display a typing indicator after responding to the user command. Bots can display a typing indicator while responding to the user command.

Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure that the bot's typing indicator is synchronized correctly with its message delivery. The typing indicator should only be visible while the bot is processing and must disappear immediately once the final response is sent. For Non‑Integrated accounts, if the bot does not show any typing indicator and replies instantly, this must be treated as a PASS scenario.

Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:
Trigger a Command: Send a standard command to the bot (e.g., "Generate Report" or "Search Database").
Monitor Typing State: Observe the chat interface for the "Bot is typing..." indicator.
Observe Response Arrival: Note when the bot's message or card appears.
Check Post-Response State: Verify that the typing indicator disappears as soon as the message is rendered.
Verify No "Ghost" Indicators: Wait 3–5 seconds to ensure the indicator does not reappear without further input.

Screenshot Capture (evidence):

FIRST, make sure you are inside the bot's 1:1 chat conversation, NOT on the Teams Apps store / app listing / install page. If the current view is the Apps catalog or the app's install/details page, click Open to enter the bot chat before capturing. The Apps store/listing page must NEVER be used as this test's evidence.
This is an interaction capture (use the deterministic capture tool): Locate the chat input field, then call capture_input_responses with input_field_id = the chat field and inputs: ["Generate Report"] (or another standard command). This types the command, submits it, waits for the bot's RESPONSE to fully render and settle, and captures the post-response state automatically.
The KEY evidence frame is the POST-RESPONSE state: after the bot's message/card has fully rendered and settled, capture ONE screenshot of the area showing the bot's response together with the input/typing area, so it is clear whether the "Bot is typing…" indicator is still present or gone.
Ghost-indicator check: After the response is captured, WAIT 3–5 seconds, let the view settle, then capture ONE additional screenshot to evidence that the typing indicator did NOT reappear without further input.
The typing indicator while the bot is still processing is allowed; if observable, you MAY capture an earlier frame showing it during processing — but the post-response and post-wait frames are mandatory and must be the LAST actions before reporting the verdict.
Capture-only guard: Evidence must show only the bot's own response and the live typing/input area in the chat. Do NOT substitute the Apps store page, the empty-state landing screen, or unrelated chat content from other test steps.
Do NOT report PASS/FAIL until BOTH the settled post-response frame AND the post-wait (ghost-check) frame have been captured.
On FAIL, ensure the settled screenshots clearly show the typing indicator persisting AFTER the response was already posted (and/or reappearing during the 3–5 second wait) as failure evidence.
Non‑Integrated note: If no typing indicator ever appears and the reply is instantaneous, capture the settled post-response frame showing the response with no indicator — this is PASS evidence.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame.
Expected Result:
The typing indicator must stop when the response is delivered. It is non‑compliant for it to continue after the message is posted. For Non‑Integrated accounts where no typing indicator appears and responses are instantaneous, the result should be PASS.
Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: "The bot sent the information card, but the typing indicator stayed visible for 5 extra seconds."
Evidence: [Insert Screenshot or Recording showing the indicator persisting]

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.7
Test Case Name: Continuous loading indicator displayed. Bots must respond to user commands without displaying a continuous loading indicator.

Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure that the bot does not leave the user in an indefinite waiting state. Any loading activity—such as the "Bot is typing..." indicator or a spinner inside a Task Module—must resolve into a final response, card, or graceful error within a reasonable timeframe (10–15 seconds). Hyperlinks present within any resulting message are allowed; if the cursor changes to a hand icon when hovered over them, this is a PASS scenario.

Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:
Trigger Complex Command: Start a command requiring significant processing (e.g., "Generate Monthly Analytics," "Sync Large Database").
Monitor Loading State: Look for the "typing" indicator or spinner UI.
Time the Duration: Observe how long the loading indicator stays visible.
Check for Resolution: Ensure the indicator stops and is replaced by either the output or a timeout message.
Test Edge Cases: If possible, test under slow network conditions or with heavy inputs to confirm a timeout or fallback response occurs instead of infinite loading.
Screenshot Capture (evidence):

FIRST, make sure you are inside the bot's 1:1 chat conversation, NOT on the Teams Apps store / app listing / install page. If the current view is the Apps catalog or the app's install/details page, click Open to enter the bot chat before capturing. The Apps store/listing page must NEVER be used as this test's evidence.
This is an interaction capture (use the deterministic capture tool): Locate the chat input field, then call capture_input_responses with input_field_id = the chat field and inputs = a processing-heavy command (e.g., ["Generate Monthly Analytics"]). This types the command, submits it, waits for the bot's RESPONSE to fully render and settle, and captures the settled outcome — so the evidence frame shows the resolved result/card/error, not the moment of typing.
LOADING-state frame (where observable): While the bot is still processing, capture ONE screenshot showing the "Bot is typing…" indicator or Task Module spinner as evidence that a loading state appeared.
RESOLUTION frame (mandatory KEY evidence): After the indicator clears, capture ONE settled screenshot showing it has resolved into the final outcome — the output card, response text, or a graceful timeout/error message. The loading indicator must NOT still be present in this frame. If the result is taller than the viewport, scroll one section at a time and capture a SEPARATE settled screenshot per position.
Persistence check (for FAIL): If the indicator does NOT resolve within the reasonable timeframe (10–15 seconds), capture a settled screenshot of the still-active loading indicator. Where possible, make the elapsed time evident (e.g., the user command timestamp visible alongside the still-spinning indicator) as proof of indefinite loading.
Capture-only guard: Every frame must show the bot's own chat response area and its live loading/resolved state. Do NOT substitute the Apps store page, the empty-state landing screen, or unrelated chat content from other test steps.
These captures must be the LAST actions before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS/FAIL until either the RESOLUTION frame (indicator cleared into a final outcome) OR the persistence frame (indicator still active past the timeframe) has been captured and settled.
On FAIL, ensure the settled screenshot clearly shows the loading indicator/spinner persisting with no resolved output as failure evidence.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame — except where the loading/persistence state IS the intended evidence, in which case capture that state deliberately.
Expected Result:
The loading indicator must not persist indefinitely. It must resolve into a final outcome or inform the user about ongoing processing. If a resulting message includes hyperlinks, hand‑cursor behavior on hover indicates valid external navigation.

Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: "The 'typing…' indicator remained active for more than 2 minutes with no response. Suggested correction: implement a backend timeout that clears the indicator and updates the user."
Example: "The Task Module spinner never cleared after data retrieval failed. Suggested correction: update the UI state with an explicit error."
Evidence: [Insert screen recording or screenshot showing persistent loading vs. elapsed time]

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.18
Test Case Name: Bot does not provide a valid response to the help command when the user has not logged into the app. Ensure your bot responds to the "help" command even if the user hasn't logged in. It must offer users a way forward or allow them to access help content related to the bot's usage.
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Verify that the bot remains responsive to the "help" command regardless of authentication status. The bot must not block help content behind login. Hyperlinks included in the help response are allowed; if the cursor changes to a hand icon when hovered over them, this indicates external redirection and is a PASS scenario.

Updated Logic

First, check whether the bot supports an in‑chat logout/sign out command and review the bot's response.
If no logout workflow is supported, the result should be WARNING (capture the bot's stated reason if available).
If logout is supported, complete the bot's logout workflow, then validate the help response while logged out (don't prematurely conclude "no help content"—evaluate the actual post‑logout help response).
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:

Check for In‑Chat Logout/Signout Support (NEW):
In personal chat, try: logout, sign out, log out, signout.
Optionally, send help or open the command menu to see if a logout option exists.
Observe:

If a logout workflow is provided → proceed to Step 2.
If the bot states logout is not supported or provides no actionable flow → mark WARNING and record the reason; stop.

Execute the Bot's Logout Workflow (If Supported):
Follow the bot's steps to sign out of the bot/app session.
Confirm the bot acknowledges logout (e.g., "You're signed out of this bot").
Ensure this is bot‑level logout (not a Teams sign‑out).

Send Help Command While Logged Out:
Type help in personal chat.
Observe whether help content is provided without forcing login.
Verify Help Content:
Ensure the response explains functionality, lists available commands, or includes a Sign In option as guidance.
Hyperlinks are allowed; if hover shows a hand cursor, treat as compliant.

Identify Dead Ends:
The bot must not stay silent.
The bot must not reply only "Please login to continue."
For Non‑Integrated accounts, a single way‑forward guidance message qualifies as PASS.

Screenshot Capture (evidence):
FIRST, make sure you are inside the bot's 1:1 chat conversation, NOT on the Teams Apps store / app listing / install page. If the current view is the Apps catalog or the app's install/details page, click Open to enter the bot chat before typing anything.
